items.question_text: When the FAA has delegated control responsibility in Canadian airspace, what basic procedural rule applies?

choices.text: Use basic FAA procedures except for the specific Canadian procedures that must be followed because of Canadian regulatory or unique operational requirements.
Use only Canadian procedures and disregard basic FAA procedures throughout the delegated airspace.
Use domestic FAA procedures only, because delegation removes the need to apply any Canadian-specific requirements.
Use whatever procedures the pilot requests, because the delegation agreement makes the two systems interchangeable.

items.explanation: Delegated Canadian airspace is generally worked with basic FAA procedures, but the chapter's Canadian-specific procedures still apply where Canadian law or unique operational requirements require them.

items.situation: You are working Canadian airspace where control responsibility has been formally delegated to the FAA. Another controller wants to treat it exactly like domestic U.S. airspace and ignore the chapter-specific Canadian procedures because the delegation means only FAA procedures apply.
items.para_context: Use normal FAA procedures as the baseline, but continue applying the Canadian procedures in this chapter where Canadian regulatory or unique operational requirements make them necessary.

choices.text: Apply basic FAA procedures, but still use the Canadian procedures in this chapter where Canadian requirements make them necessary.
Use domestic FAA procedures only, because delegated airspace eliminates the need for any Canadian-specific procedures.
Switch entirely to Canadian procedures for every action, because delegation prohibits using FAA procedures there.
Choose between FAA and Canadian procedures case by case without regard to which country's requirement actually controls the airspace.

items.explanation: Delegation does not erase Canadian-specific requirements. FAA procedures remain the base, but the chapter's required Canadian procedures still have to be applied.

items.question_text: How must ATC handle a CVFR aircraft operating in Canadian Class B airspace?

choices.text: Apply ATC procedures pertinent to IFR flights to the CVFR aircraft.
Handle it exactly like an ordinary VFR aircraft, because CVFR changes only the pilot's weather obligation.
Treat it like SVFR until it reaches the en route structure above 18,000 feet ASL.
Allow it to self-separate from IFR traffic as long as it maintains visual reference to the ground.

items.explanation: In Canadian Class B airspace, only IFR and CVFR flights are permitted, and ATC procedures pertinent to IFR flights are applied to CVFR aircraft.

items.situation: A VFR pilot wants to enter Canadian Class C airspace. Another controller says the pilot only needs to establish two-way communications before entering because that is the normal requirement for controlled VFR entry in Canada.
items.para_context: In Canadian Class C airspace, both IFR and VFR flights are permitted, but VFR flights must receive a clearance to enter. Two-way communications alone are the Class D rule, not the Class C rule.

choices.text: Require a clearance before the VFR aircraft enters the Class C airspace.
Allow entry once two-way communications are established, because that is sufficient for all controlled VFR entry in Canada.
Deny entry unless the VFR aircraft is operating as CVFR, because ordinary VFR is not permitted in Canadian Class C airspace.
Tell the pilot no clearance is needed because VFR flight in Class C has the same entry rule as Class E.

items.explanation: Canadian Class C requires a clearance for VFR entry. Two-way communications alone are not enough.

items.question_text: Which condition must be met before you clear an aircraft to maintain "at least 1,000 feet-on-top"?

choices.text: The pilot requests it, and the aircraft will not operate within Class A or Class B airspace.
The controller chooses it as the default alternative to any IFR altitude assignment, including in Class A or Class B airspace.
The pilot agrees to remain at least 1,000 feet below all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formations.
The request may be approved only if the aircraft is already operating under a CVFR clearance.

items.explanation: The clearance may be used in lieu of VFR-on-top when the pilot requests it, the weather responsibility is satisfied, and the flight will not operate within Class A or Class B airspace.

items.situation: A pilot requests "at least 1,000 feet-on-top" while planning to continue into Canadian Class B airspace. Another controller wants to approve it because the pilot asked for it and says the pilot is responsible for the weather conditions anyway.
items.para_context: The pilot may request the operation and is responsible for maintaining the required weather conditions, but the clearance is not used when the aircraft will operate within Class A or Class B airspace.

choices.text: Do not approve the request for that operation, because it may not be used in Class A or Class B airspace.
Approve it because the pilot's request alone is enough if the pilot accepts weather responsibility.
Approve it only if the pilot agrees to maintain 1,000 feet below all cloud instead of above it.
Convert the request automatically into SVFR authority for the Class B portion of the flight.

items.explanation: A pilot request and acceptable weather are not enough if the aircraft will operate in Canadian Class A or Class B airspace.

items.question_text: What separation rule applies between aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance in this airspace?

choices.text: Apply a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum regardless of the weather conditions.
Reduce the standard separation whenever the aircraft are in VMC and report each other in sight.
Use IFR separation only if both aircraft are on IFR clearances rather than CVFR.
Apply only lateral separation, because CVFR aircraft are responsible for the vertical component.

items.explanation: Aircraft on IFR or CVFR clearances are separated by lateral, longitudinal, or vertical minima regardless of the weather conditions.

items.situation: Two aircraft are operating in excellent visual conditions in Canadian airspace, one under an IFR clearance and one under a CVFR clearance. Another controller wants to reduce the standard separation because both crews report the traffic in sight and the weather is good.
items.para_context: The weather does not remove the separation requirement. Aircraft operating in accordance with IFR or CVFR clearances still receive a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum.

choices.text: Continue applying an approved lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum.
Reduce the separation because visual conditions allow the crews to assume responsibility for seeing and avoiding one another.
Treat the CVFR aircraft as ordinary VFR traffic and remove the separation requirement on that basis alone.
Use only vertical separation because lateral and longitudinal minima do not apply once both aircraft are in VMC.

items.explanation: Good weather does not eliminate the need to apply an approved separation minimum between IFR and CVFR aircraft.

items.question_text: For a turbine-powered aircraft in Canadian airspace that loses communications after departure and was cleared to a point other than its destination, what altitude plan applies unless ATC issued alternate radio-failure instructions?

choices.text: Maintain the last assigned altitude or flight level, or the MEA if higher, until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceed at the altitude or flight levels filed in the flight plan.
Maintain the clearance-limit altitude indefinitely until reaching the destination airport, regardless of the filed flight plan.
Descend immediately to the first filed cruise altitude after crossing the clearance limit.
Climb to the highest altitude shown anywhere in the route portion of the flight plan and remain there to destination.

items.explanation: The Canadian radio-failure difference for this case is the 10-minute period beyond the clearance limit before continuing at the filed altitude or flight levels.

items.situation: A turbine-powered aircraft loses communications in delegated Canadian airspace after departure and was cleared to a point other than its destination. Another controller wants to keep the aircraft at the clearance-limit altitude indefinitely until the destination is reached because that is how the radio-failure plan is being interpreted.
items.para_context: Unless alternate radio-failure instructions were issued, the aircraft maintains the last assigned altitude or the MEA if higher until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then continues at the altitude or flight levels filed in the flight plan.

choices.text: Plan control actions on the basis that the aircraft will maintain the assigned altitude or MEA until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then continue at the filed altitude or flight levels.
Assume it will remain at the clearance-limit altitude all the way to the destination unless the pilot regains communications.
Assume it will descend immediately to the lowest altitude on the route once it crosses the clearance limit.
Treat the event under ordinary U.S. radio-failure planning only and ignore the Canadian-specific difference in the chapter.

items.explanation: The Canadian-specific difference is operationally important when planning for a turbine-powered aircraft cleared to a point other than destination.

items.question_text: What authorization must exist before you authorize parachute jumping in this airspace?

choices.text: Prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority.
Only local controller approval if the jump aircraft is already airborne and the operation appears manageable.
An advisory from the destination airport, even if no Canadian authority has approved the jump.
Pilot acknowledgment that the jump is being conducted at the pilot's own risk.

items.explanation: Parachute jumping is not authorized unless prior permission has been obtained from the appropriate Canadian authority.

items.situation: A pilot requests parachute-jump approval in delegated Canadian airspace, but no prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority has been obtained. Another controller wants to approve it locally because the operation appears straightforward and the jump aircraft is already airborne.
items.para_context: Do not authorize the jump without the required prior Canadian permission. The Canadian rule is not waived just because the operation seems simple or already in progress.

choices.text: Do not authorize the parachute jump until the required Canadian permission exists.
Approve it locally because airborne status and apparent simplicity are enough to substitute for the missing permission.
Approve it if the pilot promises to obtain the written authority after landing.
Approve it as long as the pilot remains clear of IFR traffic and accepts responsibility for the operation.

items.explanation: The required Canadian permission is a prerequisite to authorizing the jump.

items.question_text: Within a Canadian control zone where an airport controller is on duty, on what basis do you approve or refuse an SVFR request?

choices.text: Current or anticipated IFR traffic only, and if approved you specify the period during which SVFR is permitted.
The pilot's IFR qualifications and the aircraft's IFR equipment status first, then any traffic concerns second.
Official ceiling and visibility reports only, because traffic is not considered when a controller is on duty.
The pilot's preferred route only, because the weather minima control the rest of the decision automatically.

items.explanation: With an airport controller on duty, the SVFR decision is based on current or anticipated IFR traffic, and an approval includes the period during which SVFR is permitted.

items.situation: A pilot requests SVFR at night in a Canadian control zone with an airport controller on duty. Another controller says the request should be denied automatically because the pilot is not IFR qualified and the aircraft is not equipped for IFR flight.
items.para_context: Canadian night SVFR does not require the pilot to be IFR qualified or the aircraft to be IFR equipped. In a control zone with an airport controller on duty, the approval decision is based on current or anticipated IFR traffic.

choices.text: Evaluate the request on the basis of current or anticipated IFR traffic, not on the pilot's IFR qualification or the aircraft's IFR equipment alone.
Deny it automatically because Canadian night SVFR requires both an IFR-qualified pilot and an IFR-equipped aircraft.
Approve it automatically because night SVFR is unrestricted whenever an airport controller is on duty.
Base the decision only on ceiling and visibility reports, because IFR traffic is irrelevant when the request is for SVFR.

items.explanation: The chapter specifically says night SVFR does not require IFR qualifications or IFR equipment, and the control decision in this setting is based on IFR traffic.
